**Indonesia**,[[b]](#cite_note-12) officially the **Republic of Indonesia**,[[c]](#cite_note-fn1-14) is a country in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"), between the [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") and [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") oceans. Comprising over [17,000 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Indonesia "List of islands of Indonesia"), including [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), and parts of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo") and [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"), Indonesia is the world's largest [archipelagic state](/wiki/Archipelagic_state "Archipelagic state") and the [14th-largest country by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"), at 1,904,569 square kilometres (735,358 square miles). Indonesia has significant areas of wilderness that support one of the [world's highest levels of biodiversity](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"). It shares [land borders](/wiki/Template:Borders_of_Indonesia "Template:Borders of Indonesia") with [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"), and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), as well as [maritime borders](/wiki/Maritime_border "Maritime border") with seven other countries, including [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").

The [Indonesian archipelago](/wiki/Nusantara_(term) "Nusantara (term)") has been inhabited since prehistoric times, with early human presence evidenced by fossils of *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* and *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")*, and megalithic sites. By the early second millennium, it had become a crossroads for international [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade") linking East and South Asia. Over the centuries, external influences—including [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Indonesia "Hinduism in Indonesia"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Indonesia "Buddhism in Indonesia") and later [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Indonesia "Islam in Indonesia")—were absorbed into local societies, which introduced lasting cultural and religious influences. European powers later competed to monopolise trade in the Spice Islands of [Maluku](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands") during the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), followed by three and a half centuries of [Dutch colonial rule](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies#History "Dutch East Indies"), before Indonesia [proclaimed its independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence") in the [aftermath of World War II](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_II "Aftermath of World War II").

Since independence, Indonesia has grappled with numerous challenges including separatism, corruption, political upheaval and natural disasters, alongside [democratisation](/wiki/Post-Suharto_era_in_Indonesia "Post-Suharto era in Indonesia") and rapid economic growth. The country today is a [presidential republic](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") with an elected [legislature](/wiki/People%27s_Consultative_Assembly "People's Consultative Assembly") and consists of [38 provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia"), some of which enjoy [greater autonomy](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") than others. Home to over 280 million people, Indonesia [ranks fourth in the world by population](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population") and has the largest [Muslim population of any country](/wiki/Islam_by_country "Islam by country"). More than half of Indonesians live on Java, the [most heavily populated island](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_population "List of islands by population") in the world, while the capital [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") is the world's [largest urban agglomeration](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities").

Indonesian society comprises hundreds of [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Indonesia "Ethnic groups in Indonesia") and [linguistic](/wiki/Languages_of_Indonesia "Languages of Indonesia") groups, with [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") forming the largest. National identity is unified under the motto *[Bhinneka Tunggal Ika](/wiki/Bhinneka_Tunggal_Ika "Bhinneka Tunggal Ika")*, reflected by a [national language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") alongside cultural and religious pluralism. A [newly industrialised country](/wiki/Newly_industrialised_country "Newly industrialised country"), Indonesia has the largest [national economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Indonesia "Economy of Indonesia") in Southeast Asia by GDP. The country plays an active role in regional and global affairs as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") and is a member of major multilateral organisations, including the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"), the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN"), and the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation").

## Etymology

Main article: [Names of Indonesia](/wiki/Names_of_Indonesia "Names of Indonesia")

The name *Indonesia* derives from the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") words *[Indos](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Indo- "wikt:Indo-")* (Ἰνδός) and [nesos](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BD%E1%BF%86%CF%83%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:νῆσος") (νῆσος), meaning 'Indian islands'.[[12]](#cite_note-EcoSeas1-15) The name dates back to the 19th century, far predating the formation of independent Indonesia. In 1850, [George Windsor Earl](/wiki/George_Windsor_Earl "George Windsor Earl"), an English [ethnologist](/wiki/Ethnology "Ethnology"), proposed the terms *Indunesians*—and, his preference, *Malayunesians*—for the inhabitants of the "Indian Archipelago or [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago")".[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarl1850119-16)[[14]](#cite_note-indoety-17) In the same publication, one of his students, [James Richardson Logan](/wiki/James_Richardson_Logan "James Richardson Logan"), used *Indonesia* as a synonym for *Indian Archipelago*.[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarl1850254,_277–278-19) Dutch academics writing in East Indies publications were reluctant to use *Indonesia*. They preferred *Malay Archipelago* ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Maleische Archipel*); the *[Netherlands East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies")* (*Nederlandsch Oost Indië*), popularly *Indië*; *the East* (*de Oost*); and *Insulinde*.[[17]](#cite_note-Kroef-20)

After 1900, *Indonesia* became more common in academic circles outside the Netherlands, and native nationalist groups adopted it for political expression.[[17]](#cite_note-Kroef-20) [Adolf Bastian](/wiki/Adolf_Bastian "Adolf Bastian") of the University of Berlin popularised the name through his book *Indonesien oder die Inseln des Malayischen Archipels, 1884–1894*. The first native scholar to use the name was [Ki Hajar Dewantara](/wiki/Ki_Hajar_Dewantara "Ki Hajar Dewantara"), who established a press bureau in the Netherlands, *Indonesisch Pers-bureau*, in 1918.[[14]](#cite_note-indoety-17)

## History

Main article: [History of Indonesia](/wiki/History_of_Indonesia "History of Indonesia")

### Early history

Main article: [Prehistoric Indonesia](/wiki/Prehistoric_Indonesia "Prehistoric Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg/250px-Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg)

One of the oldest known [figurative paintings](/wiki/Figurative_art "Figurative art"), a depiction of a bull, was discovered in the *[Lubang Jeriji Saléh](/wiki/Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h "Lubang Jeriji Saléh")* cave dated as 40,000 to 44,000 years old.

The Indonesian archipelago has been inhabited since prehistoric times. Fossils of *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* ("[Java Man](/wiki/Java_Man "Java Man")") date back between 2 million and 500,000 BCE,[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22)[[20]](#cite_note-23) while *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")* arrived around 50,000 BCE.[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) Archaeological discoveries, including [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting") in Sulawesi and Borneo and megalithic sites across Sumatra, Sulawesi, and eastern Indonesia, reflect early human settlement and ritual practices.[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)[[25]](#cite_note-Tara-28)

Around 2000 BCE, [Austronesian peoples](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") migrated into the archipelago from [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"),[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee20066-29) gradually spreading eastward and shaping much of Indonesia's linguistic and cultural foundations.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor20035–7-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) By the 8th century BCE, favourable agricultural conditions and the development of [wet-field rice cultivation](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field") supported the emergence of villages and early polities by the first century CE.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor20038–9-32) The archipelago's strategic location facilitated sustained contact with civilisations from the Indian subcontinent and mainland China, profoundly influencing Indonesian history and culture through trade.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor20033,_9–11,_13–15,_18–20,_22–23-33)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVickers20132-34)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200315–18-35)

From the 7th century, maritime kingdoms such as [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") rose to prominence on trade, adopting Hindu and Buddhist influences.[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37) Between the 8th and 10th centuries, the [Sailendra](/wiki/Sailendra "Sailendra") and [Mataram](/wiki/Mataram_Kingdom "Mataram Kingdom") dynasties left enduring architectural legacies, including [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") and [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan").[[35]](#cite_note-38) Following a failed [Mongol invasion of Java](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Java "Mongol invasion of Java"),[[36]](#cite_note-39) the [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit") empire emerged in the late 13th century, dominating much of the archipelago.[[37]](#cite_note-40) Islam [began to take root](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam_in_Indonesia "Spread of Islam in Indonesia") around the same time in northern Sumatra,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs20014,_7,_10–11-41) and gradually became the dominant religion in Java and Sumatra by the 16th century. It blended with existing local traditions, producing a distinct Islamic culture, particularly in Java.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs199112–14-42)

### Colonial era

Main articles: [Dutch East India Company in Indonesia](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company_in_Indonesia "Dutch East India Company in Indonesia") and [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Nicolaas_Pieneman_-_The_Submission_of_Prince_Dipo_Negoro_to_General_De_Kock.jpg/250px-Nicolaas_Pieneman_-_The_Submission_of_Prince_Dipo_Negoro_to_General_De_Kock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicolaas_Pieneman_-_The_Submission_of_Prince_Dipo_Negoro_to_General_De_Kock.jpg)

An 1835 painting illustrating the submission of Prince [Diponegoro](/wiki/Diponegoro "Diponegoro") to [General De Kock](/wiki/Hendrik_Merkus_de_Kock "Hendrik Merkus de Kock") at the end of the [Java War](/wiki/Java_War "Java War") in 1830

European involvement in the archipelago began in the early 16th century with [Portuguese traders seeking control of the spice trade](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire_in_the_Indonesian_Archipelago "Portuguese Empire in the Indonesian Archipelago").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs200127-43) The Dutch soon followed, establishing the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") (*Verenigde Oostindische Compagnie*, VOC) in 1602.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs200131-44) Over time, the VOC became the dominant European power until its dissolution in 1800, after which its possessions were transferred to the [Dutch state](/wiki/Batavian_Republic "Batavian Republic") as the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001144-45) Dutch control over the archipelago was tenuous and uneven,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend20038-46) facing resistance across [Java](/wiki/Java_War "Java War"), [Sumatra](/wiki/Padri_Wars "Padri Wars"), [Bali](/wiki/Template:Campaignbox_Dutch_interventions_in_Bali "Template:Campaignbox Dutch interventions in Bali"), and [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh_War "Aceh War").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarz19943–4-47)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend200321-48) Consolidation of Dutch colonial rule over Indonesia's modern boundaries was largely completed only in the early 20th century,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend20038-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2003209–278-49)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVickers20136-50) after the Dutch established posts in [western New Guinea](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea").[[48]](#cite_note-51)

[Japanese invasion](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies_campaign "Dutch East Indies campaign") and [occupation](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_the_Dutch_East_Indies "Japanese occupation of the Dutch East Indies") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") ended Dutch rule and encouraged Indonesia's independence movement.[[49]](#cite_note-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53) Shortly after [Japan's surrender](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan"), [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") and [Mohammad Hatta](/wiki/Mohammad_Hatta "Mohammad Hatta") issued the [Proclamation of Indonesian Independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence"), and they became the country's first president and vice-president, along with [Sutan Sjahrir](/wiki/Sutan_Sjahrir "Sutan Sjahrir") as Prime Minister.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001262,_268-54)[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-Bidien1945-56) The Netherlands attempted to reassert control,[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001265-57) prompting the start of [Indonesia's war of independence against the Dutch](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend200335–38-58)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001261–286-59) The conflict lasted until 1949 when the Dutch recognised Indonesian independence amid international pressure.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend200338-60)[[53]](#cite_note-Bidien1945-56)

### Post-World War II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Presiden_Sukarno_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-Presiden_Sukarno_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presiden_Sukarno_(retouched).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Mohammad_Hatta%2C_Pekan_Buku_Indonesia_1954%2C_p242.jpg/250px-Mohammad_Hatta%2C_Pekan_Buku_Indonesia_1954%2C_p242.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mohammad_Hatta,_Pekan_Buku_Indonesia_1954,_p242.jpg)

[Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") (*left*) and [Mohammad Hatta](/wiki/Mohammad_Hatta "Mohammad Hatta") (*right*), Indonesia's founding fathers and the first [president](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia") and [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Indonesia "Vice President of Indonesia") respectively

Sukarno shifted Indonesia from [democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy_period_in_Indonesia "Liberal democracy period in Indonesia") to [authoritarianism](/wiki/Guided_Democracy_in_Indonesia "Guided Democracy in Indonesia"), and maintained power by balancing the opposing forces of political Islam, [the military](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Armed_Forces "Indonesian National Armed Forces"), and the [Communist Party of Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia") (PKI).[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001312–341-61) Rising tensions culminated in an [attempted coup in 1965](/wiki/30_September_Movement "30 September Movement"), followed by a violent, widespread and military-led [anti-communist purge](/wiki/Indonesian_mass_killings_of_1965%E2%80%9366 "Indonesian mass killings of 1965–66").[[d]](#cite_note-66) The PKI was blamed for the coup and destroyed, and Sukarno's power weakened.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001342–346-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001346,_348-68) Major General [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto") [capitalised on this](/wiki/Transition_to_the_New_Order "Transition to the New Order") and assumed the presidency in 1968, establishing a US-backed ["New Order"](/wiki/New_Order_(Indonesia) "New Order (Indonesia)") military dictatorship,[[65]](#cite_note-69)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelvin20189–10-70)[[67]](#cite_note-71) which fostered [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment")[[68]](#cite_note-72)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobinson2018206-73)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBevins2020167–168-74) and drove three decades of substantial economic growth.

Indonesia's [invasion](/wiki/Indonesian_invasion_of_East_Timor "Indonesian invasion of East Timor") of [East Timor](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_East_Timor_(1975) "Democratic Republic of East Timor (1975)") in 1975 and the [subsequent occupation](/wiki/Indonesian_occupation_of_East_Timor "Indonesian occupation of East Timor") drew international condemnation,[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-ETG-76) and the regime came under growing criticism for human rights abuses after the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") ended.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVickers2013212-77)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend2003431-78) The New Order was destabilised when Indonesia became affected by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"),[[75]](#cite_note-79) bringing out [widespread discontent](/wiki/May_1998_riots_of_Indonesia#Background "May 1998 riots of Indonesia") with the New Order's corruption and political suppression and ultimately [ended Suharto's rule](/wiki/Fall_of_Suharto "Fall of Suharto").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVickers2013209–212-80)[[77]](#cite_note-81) In 1999, East Timor [voted to secede](/wiki/1999_East_Timorese_independence_referendum "1999 East Timorese independence referendum") after a 24-year military occupation that some scholars classified as [genocide](/wiki/East_Timor_genocide "East Timor genocide").[[72]](#cite_note-ETG-76)

In the [post-Suharto era since 1998](/wiki/Post-Suharto_era_in_Indonesia "Post-Suharto era in Indonesia"), Indonesia has undertaken democratic reforms including the introduction of regional autonomy and the [first direct presidential election](/wiki/2004_Indonesian_presidential_election "2004 Indonesian presidential election").[[78]](#cite_note-OtonomiDaerah-82)[[79]](#cite_note-83) Instability and [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism_in_Indonesia "Terrorism in Indonesia") were persistent in the 2000s[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85) but the economy has performed strongly since 2004 despite [pervasive corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Indonesia "Corruption in Indonesia").[[82]](#cite_note-86) Relations among the diverse population are mostly harmonious, but sectarian discontent and violence remain problematic in some areas.[[83]](#cite_note-RIP-87) A political settlement to a separatist [insurgency in Aceh](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Aceh "Insurgency in Aceh") was achieved in 2005.[[84]](#cite_note-AcehPeace-88)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Indonesia](/wiki/Geography_of_Indonesia "Geography of Indonesia") and [List of islands of Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Indonesia "List of islands of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Bromo-Semeru-Batok-Widodaren.jpg/250px-Bromo-Semeru-Batok-Widodaren.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bromo-Semeru-Batok-Widodaren.jpg)

[Mount Semeru](/wiki/Semeru "Semeru") and [Mount Bromo](/wiki/Mount_Bromo "Mount Bromo") in [East Java](/wiki/East_Java "East Java"). Indonesia's seismic and volcanic activity is among the world's highest.

Indonesia's physical geography is defined by its vast archipelagic extent and diverse landforms. It lies between latitudes [11°S](/wiki/11th_parallel_south "11th parallel south") and [6°N](/wiki/6th_parallel_north "6th parallel north") and longitudes [95°E](/wiki/95th_meridian_east "95th meridian east") and [141°E](/wiki/141st_meridian_east "141st meridian east"),[[85]](#cite_note-CCaCC-89) and is the world's largest [archipelagic state](/wiki/Archipelagic_state "Archipelagic state"), stretching 5,120 kilometres (3,181 mi) from east to west and 1,760 kilometres (1,094 mi) from north to south.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrederickWorden201198-90) The exact number of Indonesia's islands varies according to different sources, usually ranging from 13,000 to 17,000, with around 922 permanently inhabited.[[87]](#cite_note-91)[[3]](#cite_note-CIA-4) Its five main islands are [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo") (shared with Brunei and Malaysia), [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), and [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") (shared with [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea")).[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrederickWorden201199-92)

The country features diverse topography, including towering mountains, vast lakes, and extensive river systems. At 4,884 metres (16,024 ft), [Puncak Jaya](/wiki/Puncak_Jaya "Puncak Jaya") in New Guinea is Indonesia's highest peak,[[89]](#cite_note-93) while [Lake Toba](/wiki/Lake_Toba "Lake Toba") in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), covering 1,145 km2 (442 sq mi), is the largest lake.[[90]](#cite_note-94) The country's major rivers, primarily in [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan"), include [Kapuas](/wiki/Kapuas_River "Kapuas River"), [Barito](/wiki/Barito_River "Barito River") and [Mahakam](/wiki/Mahakam_River "Mahakam River"), serving as vital transportation and communication routes for remote riverine communities.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Indonesia](/wiki/Climate_of_Indonesia "Climate of Indonesia") and [Climate change in Indonesia](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Indonesia "Climate change in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Koppen-Geiger_Map_IDN_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_IDN_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_IDN_present.svg)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Indonesia[[92]](#cite_note-96)

Indonesia's climate is shaped by its equatorial position and monsoon systems. The former helps ensure a relatively stable climate year-round,[[93]](#cite_note-climateADBWBG-97) characterised by two main seasons: the [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season") from May to October and the [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") from November to April,[[93]](#cite_note-climateADBWBG-97) with no extremes of summer and winter. The climate is predominantly [tropical rainforest](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate"), with cooler climates in higher areas over 500 metres (1,600 feet) above sea level.[[85]](#cite_note-CCaCC-89)

Rainfall patterns vary across the archipelago, with regions like western Sumatra, Java, and the interiors of Kalimantan and Papua receiving more precipitation,[[94]](#cite_note-rainfall-98) while areas closer to Australia, such as Nusa Tenggara, are drier.[[94]](#cite_note-rainfall-98) The warm waters covering 81% of Indonesia's area keep land temperatures stable,[[95]](#cite_note-99) with high humidity (70–90%)[[96]](#cite_note-100) and moderate, predictable winds influenced by monsoon cycles.[[97]](#cite_note-101) Major weather hazards include strong currents in straits, such as the [Lombok](/wiki/Lombok_Strait "Lombok Strait") and [Sape Straits](/wiki/Sape_Strait "Sape Strait"),[[98]](#cite_note-102) rather than [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone").[[99]](#cite_note-103)

Several studies consider Indonesia to be at severe risk from the [projected effects of climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Indonesia "Climate change in Indonesia").[[100]](#cite_note-104) A temperature rise of 1.5 °C (3 °F)[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-ClimChng-106) could intensify droughts, disrupt rainfall patterns critical to agriculture,[[102]](#cite_note-ClimChng-106) and increase occurrences of food shortages, diseases, and wildfires.[[102]](#cite_note-ClimChng-106) [Rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") would threaten densely populated coastal regions,[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108) particularly given Indonesia's extensive coastlines. Impoverished communities are expected to be disproportionately affected.[[105]](#cite_note-109)

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Indonesia](/wiki/Geology_of_Indonesia "Geology of Indonesia")

See also: [List of volcanoes in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_volcanoes_in_Indonesia "List of volcanoes in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Lake_Toba_and_the_surrounding_hills.jpg/250px-Lake_Toba_and_the_surrounding_hills.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Toba_and_the_surrounding_hills.jpg)

[Lake Toba](/wiki/Lake_Toba "Lake Toba") in [North Sumatra](/wiki/North_Sumatra "North Sumatra"), the world's largest known [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic") caldera.[[106]](#cite_note-110)

Indonesia's geology is shaped by its position on the Pacific [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire"), namely a subduction zone where several tectonic plates collide.[[e]](#cite_note-111)[[107]](#cite_note-volcano-112) This tectonic activity makes the region highly unstable with volcanoes and earthquakes.[[107]](#cite_note-volcano-112) Around 130 volcanoes are classified as active,[[107]](#cite_note-volcano-112) stretching from [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") through [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali") and the [Lesser Sunda Islands](/wiki/Lesser_Sunda_Islands "Lesser Sunda Islands"), and the [Banda Islands](/wiki/Banda_Islands "Banda Islands") to northeastern [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitton200338-113) [Volcanic ash](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash"), while harmful to agriculture, has created fertile soils that have historically sustained the high population densities of regions with volcanoes,[[109]](#cite_note-114) such as Java and Bali.

The archipelago has witnessed several powerful volcanic eruptions with global repercussions. A [massive supervolcano erupted](/wiki/Youngest_Toba_eruption#Toba_catastrophe_theory "Youngest Toba eruption") at present-day [Lake Toba](/wiki/Lake_Toba "Lake Toba") around 74,000 BCE, which possibly influenced human evolution.[[110]](#cite_note-115) The [eruption of Mount Tambora in 1815](/wiki/1815_eruption_of_Mount_Tambora "1815 eruption of Mount Tambora") had global climatic effects, making much of the Northern Hemisphere [without summer in 1816](/wiki/Year_Without_a_Summer "Year Without a Summer"),[[111]](#cite_note-116) while the [eruption of Mount Krakatoa in 1883](/wiki/1883_eruption_of_Krakatoa "1883 eruption of Krakatoa") produced the loudest sound in recorded history, with additional worldwide impacts.[[112]](#cite_note-117) Recent catastrophic disasters due to seismic activity include the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake") and the [2018 Sulawesi earthquake](/wiki/2018_Sulawesi_earthquake_and_tsunami "2018 Sulawesi earthquake and tsunami").[[113]](#cite_note-118)

### Biodiversity

Main articles: [Fauna of Indonesia](/wiki/Fauna_of_Indonesia "Fauna of Indonesia") and [Flora of Indonesia](/wiki/Flora_of_Indonesia "Flora of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/202306_Varanus_komodoensis.jpg/250px-202306_Varanus_komodoensis.jpg)](/wiki/File:202306_Varanus_komodoensis.jpg)

The [Komodo dragon](/wiki/Komodo_dragon "Komodo dragon") (*Varanus komodoensis*) is the [official national animal of Indonesia](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Indonesia "National symbols of Indonesia")

Recognised by [Conservation International](/wiki/Conservation_International "Conservation International") as one of 17 [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"),[[114]](#cite_note-119) Indonesia hosts one of the world's highest levels of biodiversity due to its tropical climate, large size, and archipelagic geography. The country's flora and fauna include a mix of Asian and [Australasian](/wiki/Australasian_realm "Australasian realm") species.[[115]](#cite_note-120) The [Sunda Shelf](/wiki/Sunda_Shelf "Sunda Shelf") islands (Sumatra, Java, and Borneo) have a wealth of Asian fauna as they were once linked to mainland Asia,[[116]](#cite_note-121)[[117]](#cite_note-122) while Sulawesi, Lesser Sunda Islands, Maluku and Papua evolved unique ecosystems due to their separation from the continental landmasses.[[118]](#cite_note-123)[[119]](#cite_note-124)

Indonesia boasts 54,716 kilometres (33,999 miles) of coastline,[[3]](#cite_note-CIA-4) featuring extensive coastal and marine ecosystems, such as dunes and mangroves,[[12]](#cite_note-EcoSeas1-15) as well as coral reefs in the [Coral Triangle](/wiki/Coral_Triangle "Coral Triangle") that harbour the highest diversity of [coral reef fish](/wiki/Coral_reef_fish "Coral reef fish") globally.[[120]](#cite_note-125) The [Wallace Line](/wiki/Wallace_Line "Wallace Line") marks the biogeographical divide between Asian and Australasian species, with the region between the Wallace and [Weber Lines](/wiki/Weber_Line "Weber Line") (called [Wallacea](/wiki/Wallacea "Wallacea")) hosting particularly high levels of endemic biodiversity.[[121]](#cite_note-126)[[122]](#cite_note-127)[[123]](#cite_note-Severin-128) In addition, 83% of Southeast Asia's old-growth forests are located in Indonesia.[[124]](#cite_note-129)

### Environment and conservation

Main articles: [Environment of Indonesia](/wiki/Environment_of_Indonesia "Environment of Indonesia") and [Conservation in Indonesia](/wiki/Conservation_in_Indonesia "Conservation in Indonesia")

See also: [Protected areas of Indonesia](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Indonesia "Protected areas of Indonesia"), [List of national parks of Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Indonesia "List of national parks of Indonesia"), and [List of biosphere reserves of Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_biosphere_reserves_of_Indonesia "List of biosphere reserves of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Unnamed_Road%2C_Kapisawar%2C_Meos_Mansar%2C_Kabupaten_Raja_Ampat%2C_Papua_Bar._98482%2C_Indonesia_-_panoramio_%2884%29.jpg/250px-Unnamed_Road%2C_Kapisawar%2C_Meos_Mansar%2C_Kabupaten_Raja_Ampat%2C_Papua_Bar._98482%2C_Indonesia_-_panoramio_%2884%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unnamed_Road,_Kapisawar,_Meos_Mansar,_Kabupaten_Raja_Ampat,_Papua_Bar._98482,_Indonesia_-_panoramio_(84).jpg)

[Clown fish](/wiki/Clown_fish "Clown fish") in coral of [Raja Ampat islands](/wiki/Raja_Ampat_islands "Raja Ampat islands"), one of Indonesia's over 100 marine protected areas

Indonesia experiences significant [environmental challenges](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Indonesia "Environmental issues in Indonesia") due to peatland degradation, deforestation[[125]](#cite_note-deforest-130)[[126]](#cite_note-131)[[f]](#cite_note-134) and the overexploitation of natural resources associated with industries such as logging and plantation agriculture,[[127]](#cite_note-landuse-132) particularly palm oil cultivation.[[129]](#cite_note-135)[[130]](#cite_note-136) This situation threatens indigenous and endemic species, including the critically endangered [Bali myna](/wiki/Bali_myna "Bali myna"),[[131]](#cite_note-137) [Sumatran orangutan](/wiki/Sumatran_orangutan "Sumatran orangutan"),[[132]](#cite_note-138) and [Javan rhinoceros](/wiki/Javan_rhinoceros "Javan rhinoceros"),[[133]](#cite_note-139) and it is often given a lower priority due to high poverty levels and weak, under-resourced governance.[[134]](#cite_note-140) In academic discourse, some scholars have labelled the level of environmental degradation in Indonesia as ecocide.[[135]](#cite_note-141)[[136]](#cite_note-142)

In response, Indonesia has designated 27 million hectares (14% of the land area) as [protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Indonesia "Protected areas of Indonesia")[[137]](#cite_note-prtc-143) as of 2020, and has established an extensive network of marine reserves.[[138]](#cite_note-144) Its conservation framework includes 54 national parks, several of which are recognised as [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites"), [World Network of Biosphere Reserves](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves"), and wetlands of international importance under the 1971 [Ramsar Convention](/wiki/Ramsar_Convention "Ramsar Convention").[[139]](#cite_note-145) These protected areas encompass a wide range of ecosystems, including tropical rainforests and mangroves, and are intended to safeguard biodiversity while supporting sustainable resource use and local livelihoods.[[140]](#cite_note-RIO-146)

Despite these measures, conservation efforts are hindered by persistent obstacles.[[141]](#cite_note-CCII-147) Studies have noted that enforcement and management capacity vary across regions,[[137]](#cite_note-prtc-143) while pressures from deforestation, land-use change, and resource exploitation continue to affect ecosystems and wildlife populations.[[142]](#cite_note-148) Habitat loss and environmental degradation have contributed to the decline of numerous species, including several classified as threatened or endangered.[[140]](#cite_note-RIO-146)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Indonesia](/wiki/Politics_of_Indonesia "Politics of Indonesia")

See also: [Government of Indonesia](/wiki/Government_of_Indonesia "Government of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Ruang_MPR.jpg/250px-Ruang_MPR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruang_MPR.jpg)

A presidential inauguration by the MPR in the [Parliament Complex](/wiki/MPR/DPR/DPD_building "MPR/DPR/DPD building") Jakarta, [2014](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Joko_Widodo "First inauguration of Joko Widodo")

Indonesia is governed as a [presidential republic](/wiki/Presidential_republic "Presidential republic") under a constitutional framework that defines its political institutions. Following the [fall of the New Order in 1998](/wiki/Fall_of_Suharto "Fall of Suharto"), sweeping [amendments to the constitution](/wiki/Amendments_to_the_Constitution_of_Indonesia "Amendments to the Constitution of Indonesia") restructured the state's executive, legislative, and judicial branches. The reforms preserved Indonesia’s [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") framework while expanding decentralisation to regional governments.[[143]](#cite_note-Harijanti2006-149)[[144]](#cite_note-150) The [president](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia") serves as [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and commander-in-chief of the [Indonesian National Armed Forces](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Armed_Forces "Indonesian National Armed Forces") (*Tentara Nasional Indonesia*, TNI) and oversees domestic and foreign policies. Presidents may serve up to two consecutive five-year terms.[[145]](#cite_note-151)

Legislative authority is vested in the [People's Consultative Assembly](/wiki/People%27s_Consultative_Assembly "People's Consultative Assembly") (*Majelis Permusyawaratan Rakyat*, MPR), Indonesia's highest representative body that is responsible for amending the constitution, inaugurating and impeaching the president, and formalising state policies.[[146]](#cite_note-152)[[11]](#cite_note-UUD45-13) It consists of two houses: the [People's Representative Council](/wiki/People%27s_Representative_Council "People's Representative Council") (*Dewan Perwakilan Rakyat*, DPR), which has 580 members and handles legislation and executive oversight, and the [Regional Representative Council](/wiki/Regional_Representative_Council "Regional Representative Council") (*Dewan Perwakilan Daerah*, DPD), which has 152 members and focuses on regional matters.[[147]](#cite_note-INAlegis-153) Since 1998, reforms have expanded the governance role of the DPR.[[143]](#cite_note-Harijanti2006-149)

[Judicial authority](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Indonesia "Judiciary of Indonesia") is exercised by several key institutions. The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Indonesia "Supreme Court of Indonesia") (*Mahkamah Agung*) is the highest judicial body, handling final appeals and case reviews.[[148]](#cite_note-courts-154) The [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Indonesia "Constitutional Court of Indonesia") (*Mahkamah Konstitusi*) adjudicates constitutional and political matters,[[148]](#cite_note-courts-154) while the country's Religious Court (*Pengadilan Agama*) oversees [Islamic](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") personal law cases.[[149]](#cite_note-155) Additionally, the [Judicial Commission](/wiki/Judicial_Commission_of_Indonesia "Judicial Commission of Indonesia") (*Komisi Yudisial*) monitors judicial performance, and works to uphold the integrity and independence of the judiciary.[[150]](#cite_note-156)

### Parties and elections

Main articles: [List of political parties in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Indonesia "List of political parties in Indonesia") and [Elections in Indonesia](/wiki/Elections_in_Indonesia "Elections in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Prabowo_Subianto_2024_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Prabowo_Subianto_2024_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prabowo_Subianto_2024_official_portrait.jpg)

[Prabowo Subianto](/wiki/Prabowo_Subianto "Prabowo Subianto"),  
 8th [president of Indonesia](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Gibran_Rakabuming_2024_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Gibran_Rakabuming_2024_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gibran_Rakabuming_2024_official_portrait.jpg)

[Gibran Rakabuming Raka](/wiki/Gibran_Rakabuming_Raka "Gibran Rakabuming Raka"),  
 14th [vice president of Indonesia](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Indonesia "Vice President of Indonesia")

Since 1999, electoral politics in Indonesia have been characterised by a competitive [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") in which no party has secured an outright majority of seats in the [legislative elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Indonesia "Elections in Indonesia").[[151]](#cite_note-election-157) Political parties are commonly classified as secular-nationalist or Islamic-oriented,[[g]](#cite_note-158)[[152]](#cite_note-159) though political competition is typically shaped less by ideological commitments than by pragmatic coalition-building suited to prevailing political conditions.[[153]](#cite_note-ParPol-160) [Power sharing among parties](/wiki/Cartel_party_theory "Cartel party theory") is widespread,[[154]](#cite_note-161) and governing coalitions are often oversized.[[151]](#cite_note-election-157) Unlike in many democracies, parties frequently establish alliances before elections rather than afterward.[[153]](#cite_note-ParPol-160)

Indonesia held its first [general election in 1955](/wiki/1955_Indonesian_legislative_election "1955 Indonesian legislative election"), and since [2004](/wiki/2004_Indonesian_presidential_election "2004 Indonesian presidential election"), the president and the legislature has been directly elected for a five-year term. Members of the DPR are elected through party-based contests, while members of the DPD are elected on a non-partisan basis to represent provincial constituencies.[[147]](#cite_note-INAlegis-153)[[143]](#cite_note-Harijanti2006-149) Indonesia’s archipelagic geography and dispersed population make its national elections among the most logistically complex in the world. Ballots and electoral materials must be transported by land, sea, and air to remote islands, mountainous areas, and isolated communities.[[155]](#cite_note-162)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Subdivisions of Indonesia](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Indonesia "Subdivisions of Indonesia")

Indonesia is administratively divided into several levels of regional government. At the highest subnational level are [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia"), each governed by an elected [governor](/wiki/List_of_current_governors_in_Indonesia "List of current governors in Indonesia") (*gubernur*) and a provincial legislature (*Dewan Perwakilan Rakyat Daerah*, DPRD). Provinces are subdivided into [regencies](/wiki/Regency_(Indonesia) "Regency (Indonesia)") (*kabupaten*) and cities (*kota*), headed by elected regents (*bupati*) and mayors (*wali kota*) and supported by local legislatures (*DPRD Kabupaten/Kota*); since the implementation of regional autonomy in 1999, these units have served as the primary administrative level responsible for most public services.[[78]](#cite_note-OtonomiDaerah-82) Below regencies and cities are [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Indonesia "Districts of Indonesia") (*kecamatan*), which in turn are subdivided into [villages](/wiki/Villages_of_Indonesia "Villages of Indonesia"),[[156]](#cite_note-villages-163) the lowest level of administration, comprising self-governing rural villages (*desa*) and administratively subordinate urban villages (*kelurahan*).[[157]](#cite_note-164)

Villages are further organised into neighbourhood and community associations (*rukun tetangga*, RT, and *rukun warga*, RW),[[158]](#cite_note-165) with additional local subdivisions such as hamlets (*dusun* or *dukuh*) in Java.[[156]](#cite_note-villages-163) Nine provinces possess [special autonomous status](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") reflecting historical, cultural, or political circumstances. These include [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh "Aceh"), which has the authority to implement aspects of Islamic law;[[159]](#cite_note-166) [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), whose designation stems from its role as the national capital;[[160]](#cite_note-167) and [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta"), which retains a hereditary sultanate within the republican system.[[161]](#cite_note-168) Special autonomy is also granted to the six provinces in [Papua](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea#Current_government "Western New Guinea") to increase local self-governance and address concerns of separatism and development.[[162]](#cite_note-169)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Indonesia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Indonesia "Foreign relations of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/ASEAN_HQ_1.jpg/250px-ASEAN_HQ_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:ASEAN_HQ_1.jpg)

Jakarta hosts the headquarters of ASEAN and functions as the organisation's main diplomatic hub.[[163]](#cite_note-170)

Indonesia follows an "independent and active" (*bebas aktif*) foreign policy, a term coined in 1948 by the country's first vice-president, [Mohammad Hatta](/wiki/Mohammad_Hatta "Mohammad Hatta").[[164]](#cite_note-171) With this policy, the country aims to navigate great power politics, maintain autonomy, and avoid alignment.[[165]](#cite_note-172) Foreign policy is directed by the president[[166]](#cite_note-173)[[11]](#cite_note-UUD45-13) and implemented by the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Indonesia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Indonesia)"),[[167]](#cite_note-174) with the parliament providing a role in oversight.[[168]](#cite_note-175) Indonesia is considered to be a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") in global politics.[[169]](#cite_note-176)

As the largest country in Southeast Asia and a founding member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations ([ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")), Indonesia considers ASEAN the cornerstone of its foreign policy.[[170]](#cite_note-177) Outside of its immediate region, Indonesia has [actively supported Palestine](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Palestine_relations "Indonesia–Palestine relations")[[171]](#cite_note-178) while refraining from formal [diplomatic relations with Israel](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Indonesia–Israel relations"), although discreet ties exist.[[172]](#cite_note-179) Since the start of the 21st century, Indonesia has developed close [relations with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Indonesia_relations "China–Indonesia relations"), primarily relating to trade and investments in infrastructure,[[173]](#cite_note-180) while at the same time maintaining a strategic [partnership with the United States](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Indonesia–United States relations"), focusing on economic cooperation, security, and counterterrorism efforts.[[174]](#cite_note-181)

Indonesia has been [a member of the United Nations](/wiki/Indonesia_and_the_United_Nations "Indonesia and the United Nations") since 1950, with a brief interruption in 1965,[[h]](#cite_note-fn2-183) and is a founding member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") (NAM), the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") (OIC) and the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit").[[176]](#cite_note-184) Indonesia also participates in multilateral economic institutions, including [APEC](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") and the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO). The country has been a humanitarian and development aid recipient since the late 1960s,[[177]](#cite_note-185) but has established its own foreign aid agency in 2019.[[178]](#cite_note-186) It also plays a role in maintaining international peace and security, deploying military and police personnel to multiple UN peacekeeping missions since 1957, including [Lebanon](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Force_in_Lebanon "United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon"), the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/MONUSCO "MONUSCO") and [Mali](/wiki/United_Nations_Multidimensional_Integrated_Stabilization_Mission_in_Mali "United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali").[[179]](#cite_note-187)

### Military

Main articles: [Indonesian National Armed Forces](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Armed_Forces "Indonesian National Armed Forces") and [Military history of Indonesia](/wiki/Military_history_of_Indonesia "Military history of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Taruna_akmil.jpg/250px-Taruna_akmil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taruna_akmil.jpg)

[Indonesian Military Academy](/wiki/Indonesian_Military_Academy "Indonesian Military Academy") cadets

The [Indonesian National Armed Forces](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Armed_Forces "Indonesian National Armed Forces") (TNI) consists of the [Army](/wiki/Indonesian_Army "Indonesian Army") (TNI-AD), [Navy](/wiki/Indonesian_Navy "Indonesian Navy") (TNI-AL) (including the [Marine Corps](/wiki/Indonesian_Marine_Corps "Indonesian Marine Corps")), and [Air Force](/wiki/Indonesian_Air_Force "Indonesian Air Force") (TNI-AU), with active personnel numbering approximately 300,400 in the Army, 65,000 in the Navy, and 30,100 in the Air Force.[[180]](#cite_note-188) Established during the [Indonesian National Revolution](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution"), the TNI initially engaged in guerrilla warfare alongside informal militias,[[181]](#cite_note-189) and eventually developed a territorial structure that focuses on maintaining domestic stability and deterring foreign threats.[[182]](#cite_note-12YMR-190)

During the New Order, the military exercised a direct political role under a doctrine known as "dual function" (*[dwifungsi](/wiki/Dwifungsi "Dwifungsi")*).[[183]](#cite_note-191) Reforms in 1998 removed the military's overt political involvement, although it continued to retain political influence.[[182]](#cite_note-12YMR-190)[[184]](#cite_note-192) In addition, the military's involvement in commercial enterprises has drawn scrutiny.[[185]](#cite_note-193) Defence spending has remained below 1% since 2007,[[186]](#cite_note-194) a relatively modest allocation for a country of Indonesia's geographic scale and strategic challenges.[[187]](#cite_note-195)

Since independence, Indonesia has faced separatist movements and insurgencies, notably in [Aceh](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Aceh "Insurgency in Aceh") and [Papua](/wiki/Papuan_conflict "Papuan conflict").[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend2003269–273,_477–480-196)[[189]](#cite_note-197) While the former ended in 2005,[[84]](#cite_note-AcehPeace-88) the latter has continued alongside implementation of regional autonomy[[190]](#cite_note-198) and well-documented human rights abuses by the TNI.[[i]](#cite_note-200) Indonesia's historical military engagements include [conflicts with the Netherlands](/wiki/West_New_Guinea_dispute "West New Guinea dispute") over [Dutch New Guinea](/wiki/Dutch_New_Guinea "Dutch New Guinea"), opposition to the British-backed creation of Malaysia (*[Konfrontasi](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation")*), the anti-communist mass killings, and the [invasion of East Timor](/wiki/Indonesian_invasion_of_East_Timor "Indonesian invasion of East Timor"), which was Indonesia's largest military operation.[[192]](#cite_note-201)[[193]](#cite_note-202)

### Law enforcement and human rights

Main articles: [Indonesian National Police](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Police "Indonesian National Police") and [Human rights in Indonesia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Indonesia "Human rights in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Jakarta_riot_14_May_1998.jpg/250px-Jakarta_riot_14_May_1998.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_riot_14_May_1998.jpg)

Riots on the streets of Jakarta on 14 May 1998, part of a [wave of civil unrest](/wiki/May_1998_Indonesia_riots "May 1998 Indonesia riots") that involved attacks on property and individuals associated with the ethnic Chinese community.

Law enforcement in Indonesia is primarily carried out by the [Indonesian National Police](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Police "Indonesian National Police") (POLRI), which operates under the direct authority of the President. POLRI is tasked with law enforcement and maintaining public order and security, alongside various other agencies, supervised and trained by POLRI, that perform policing functions for specific public services.[[194]](#cite_note-203)

The exercise of law enforcement and state authority has intersected with longstanding human rights concerns. Indonesia has a documented history of racial discrimination and conflicts, affecting [Chinese Indonesians](/wiki/Discrimination_against_Chinese_Indonesians "Discrimination against Chinese Indonesians"), [Papuans](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_New_Guinea "Indigenous people of New Guinea"),[[195]](#cite_note-204)[[196]](#cite_note-205) and populations impacted by the government's [transmigration program](/wiki/Transmigration_program "Transmigration program").[[197]](#cite_note-206) Other minorities, including religious minorities and [LGBTQ individuals](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Indonesia "LGBTQ rights in Indonesia"), also experience discrimination and social hostility.[[83]](#cite_note-RIP-87)[[198]](#cite_note-mino-207)[[199]](#cite_note-208)[[200]](#cite_note-209)

Human rights oversight in Indonesia is shaped by social, historical, and institutional factors, with varying outcomes across regions. The [National Commission on Human Rights](/wiki/National_Commission_on_Human_Rights "National Commission on Human Rights") (*Komnas HAM*), established in 1993, serves as the primary independent body responsible for monitoring and investigating abuses.[[201]](#cite_note-210) The commission is generally regarded as an important institutional safeguard, though observers note limitations in its authority and capacity to ensure compliance.[[202]](#cite_note-211)

## Economy

Main articles: [Economy of Indonesia](/wiki/Economy_of_Indonesia "Economy of Indonesia"), [Agriculture in Indonesia](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Indonesia "Agriculture in Indonesia"), and [Poverty in Indonesia](/wiki/Poverty_in_Indonesia "Poverty in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Perkebunan_kelapa_sawit_milik_rakyat_%281%29.JPG/250px-Perkebunan_kelapa_sawit_milik_rakyat_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Perkebunan_kelapa_sawit_milik_rakyat_(1).JPG)

[Palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil") plantation in [Kampar Regency](/wiki/Kampar_Regency "Kampar Regency"), [Riau](/wiki/Riau "Riau"). Indonesia is the world's largest producer of palm oil.[[203]](#cite_note-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Morowali_Industrial_Park.jpg/250px-Morowali_Industrial_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morowali_Industrial_Park.jpg)

[Morowali Industrial Park](/wiki/Morowali_Industrial_Park "Morowali Industrial Park") hosting primarily [nickel-related industries](/wiki/Nickel_mining_in_Indonesia "Nickel mining in Indonesia") in [Morowali Regency](/wiki/Morowali_Regency "Morowali Regency"), [Central Sulawesi](/wiki/Central_Sulawesi "Central Sulawesi"). Indonesia is the world's largest producer of nickel.[[204]](#cite_note-213)

Indonesia operates a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") where the private sector and the government play significant roles.[[205]](#cite_note-214) As the only [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20") member state in Southeast Asia,[[206]](#cite_note-215) it has the region's largest economy by GDP (ranking inside [top 20 in nominal terms](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") and [top 10 by purchasing power parity](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)")) and is classified as a [newly industrialised country](/wiki/Newly_industrialised_country "Newly industrialised country"). In recent years, services and industry have accounted for the largest shares of gross domestic product, while agriculture remains a major source of employment,[[207]](#cite_note-216) particularly outside urban centres.

The structure of the economy has [shifted considerably since independence](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Indonesia "Economic history of Indonesia"). It was initially mostly agrarian before undergoing industrialisation and urbanisation from the late 1960s.[[208]](#cite_note-217) Economic diversification accelerated in the 1980s and 1990s as manufactured exports expanded, contributing to rapid growth and poverty reduction.[[209]](#cite_note-218)[[210]](#cite_note-219) This growth was interrupted by the [Asian financial crisis of the late 1990s](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"), which caused a sharp economic contraction.[[211]](#cite_note-220) Since the early 2000s, a combination of banking reforms,[[212]](#cite_note-08GFC-221)[[213]](#cite_note-222) fiscal discipline, and flexible exchange-rate policies has supported a steady recovery, with growth rates generally remaining stable in the following two decades,[[214]](#cite_note-223) including after the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[212]](#cite_note-08GFC-221)[[215]](#cite_note-224)

Despite sustained growth, a range of structural constraints remain, including uneven regional development,[[216]](#cite_note-225)[[217]](#cite_note-226) a large informal sector,[[218]](#cite_note-227) low productivity,[[219]](#cite_note-228) infrastructure limitations,[[220]](#cite_note-229) and regulatory and governance hurdles.[[221]](#cite_note-230) Indonesia possesses abundant resources that continue to shape its economy. Its extractive industries produce commodities such as coal, nickel, petroleum, and natural gas,[[222]](#cite_note-OEC-231) while its agricultural sector is a major global supplier of products including palm oil, coffee, and spices.[[223]](#cite_note-232) The country also imports refined petroleum products and industrial inputs, and its major trade partners are primarily in Asia, alongside the United States.[[222]](#cite_note-OEC-231)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Indonesia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Indonesia "Tourism in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Borobudur-Nothwest-view.jpg/330px-Borobudur-Nothwest-view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borobudur-Nothwest-view.jpg)

[Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") in [Central Java](/wiki/Central_Java "Central Java"), the world's largest Buddhist [pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid "Pyramid") temple, is the single most visited tourist attraction in Indonesia.[[224]](#cite_note-233)

Tourism is a significant source of revenue to the economy, drawing on Indonesia's natural landscapes and cultural heritage. In 2023, it contributed [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")14 billion to GDP and drew 11.6 million international visitors.[[225]](#cite_note-234) Australia, Singapore, Malaysia, China, and India are among the top five sources of visitors to Indonesia.[[226]](#cite_note-235)

The country is renowned for its rich and diverse natural ecosystems, with forests covering 47.7% of its land area.[[128]](#cite_note-forestcov-133) Popular natural destinations include the rainforests of Sumatra and Kalimantan, particularly the Orangutan wildlife reserves.[[227]](#cite_note-236) Indonesia also has one of the world's longest coastlines, stretching 54,716 kilometres (33,999 mi).[[3]](#cite_note-CIA-4) Cultural tourism features prominently, with attractions like the ancient [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") and [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") temples,[[228]](#cite_note-237) the [Toraja](/wiki/Toraja "Toraja") highlands,[[229]](#cite_note-238) and the cultural festivals of [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali").[[230]](#cite_note-239)

Indonesia is home to [ten official UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Indonesia "List of World Heritage Sites in Indonesia"), including [Komodo National Park](/wiki/Komodo_National_Park "Komodo National Park") and the [Cosmological Axis of Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta").[[231]](#cite_note-UNESCOWHC-240) Additionally, 21 other sites are on the tentative list, such as [Bunaken National Park](/wiki/Bunaken_National_Park "Bunaken National Park") and the [Raja Ampat Islands](/wiki/Raja_Ampat_Islands "Raja Ampat Islands").[[231]](#cite_note-UNESCOWHC-240) Historical tourism is also a major draw, with attractions like the colonial heritage of the Dutch East Indies in [Jakarta](/wiki/Kota_Tua_Jakarta "Kota Tua Jakarta") and [Semarang](/wiki/Dutch_architecture_in_Semarang "Dutch architecture in Semarang"),[[232]](#cite_note-241)[[233]](#cite_note-242) as well as the [royal palaces](/wiki/List_of_palaces_in_Indonesia "List of palaces in Indonesia") of [Pagaruyung](/wiki/Pagaruyung_Palace "Pagaruyung Palace") and [Ubud](/wiki/Ubud_Palace "Ubud Palace").[[234]](#cite_note-243)[[235]](#cite_note-244)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Indonesia](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Indonesia "Science and technology in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/STS-41-B_Palapa_B-2_deployment.jpg/250px-STS-41-B_Palapa_B-2_deployment.jpg)](/wiki/File:STS-41-B_Palapa_B-2_deployment.jpg)

[Palapa](/wiki/Palapa "Palapa") satellite launch in 1984

Government spending on research and development in Indonesia has historically been a small proportion of national expenditure,[[236]](#cite_note-245) which has shaped the pace and scale of scientific and technological development.[[237]](#cite_note-246) Historical innovations include the *[pinisi](/wiki/Pinisi "Pinisi")* boats of the [Bugis](/wiki/Bugis "Bugis") and [Makassar people](/wiki/Makassar_people "Makassar people"),[[238]](#cite_note-247) while the most recent ones include the [Sosrobahu](/wiki/Sosrobahu "Sosrobahu") road construction technique that has been used internationally.[[239]](#cite_note-248)[[240]](#cite_note-249)

Indonesia is one of the few developing countries with an aircraft manufacturing industry.[[241]](#cite_note-Steenhuis-250) The state-owned [Indonesian Aerospace](/wiki/Indonesian_Aerospace "Indonesian Aerospace") (*PT. Dirgantara Indonesia*, PTDI) has domestically produced the [N219](/wiki/Indonesian_Aerospace_N219 "Indonesian Aerospace N219"),[[242]](#cite_note-251) collaborated with Spain's [CASA](/wiki/CASA_(aircraft_manufacturer) "CASA (aircraft manufacturer)") to develop the [CN-235](/wiki/CASA/IPTN_CN-235 "CASA/IPTN CN-235"),[[241]](#cite_note-Steenhuis-250) and supplied components to [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing") and [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus").[[243]](#cite_note-252) Beyond aerospace, another state-led industrial capability is evident in rail manufacturing, where the country produces passenger trains and freight wagons through the [Indonesian Railway Industry](/wiki/Industri_Kereta_Api "Industri Kereta Api") (*Industri Kereta Api*, INKA), which exports trains to international markets, including Africa and New Zealand.[[244]](#cite_note-253)

Indonesia is one of the first countries in Asia to build a space program.[[245]](#cite_note-actaastro-254) The [National Institute of Aeronautics and Space](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Aeronautics_and_Space "National Institute of Aeronautics and Space") (*Lembaga Penerbangan dan Antariksa Nasional*, LAPAN), founded in 1963, oversaw Indonesia's space program until [2021](/wiki/Research_Organization_for_Aeronautics_and_Space "Research Organization for Aeronautics and Space"). Indonesia was the first developing country with a satellite system when LAPAN launched *[Palapa](/wiki/Palapa "Palapa")* in 1976 with U.S. assistance.[[245]](#cite_note-actaastro-254) Since 2003, LAPAN has developed and launched [micro-satellites](/wiki/Lapan-TUBsat "Lapan-TUBsat") for Earth-observation, remote sensing and maritime monitoring,[[246]](#cite_note-255)[[247]](#cite_note-256) and has conducted [suborbital rocket tests](/wiki/RX_(rocket_family) "RX (rocket family)"),[[248]](#cite_note-257) at the [Pameungpeuk launch site](/wiki/Stasiun_Peluncuran_Roket "Stasiun Peluncuran Roket") in [Garut Regency](/wiki/Garut_Regency "Garut Regency"), West Java.

## Infrastructure

See also: [List of main infrastructure projects in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_main_infrastructure_projects_in_Indonesia "List of main infrastructure projects in Indonesia")

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Indonesia](/wiki/Transport_in_Indonesia "Transport in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Whoosh_High-speed_Train_G1224%2C_in_Bojongkoneng%2C_Ngamprah.jpg/250px-Whoosh_High-speed_Train_G1224%2C_in_Bojongkoneng%2C_Ngamprah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whoosh_High-speed_Train_G1224,_in_Bojongkoneng,_Ngamprah.jpg)

*Whoosh* is the first high-speed rail in Southeast Asia and Southern Hemisphere. It is expected to be extended to the country's second largest city, [Surabaya](/wiki/Surabaya "Surabaya").[[249]](#cite_note-railways-258)

Indonesia's transport system reflects its archipelagic geography and population concentration on Java.[[250]](#cite_note-259) Roads form the backbone of land transport,[[251]](#cite_note-260) and public bus networks support mobility in most large cities. [Transjakarta](/wiki/Transjakarta "Transjakarta") operates one of the world's largest bus rapid transit systems,[[252]](#cite_note-261) while [ride-hailing services](/wiki/Ridesharing_company "Ridesharing company")[[253]](#cite_note-262) and informal transport modes supplement conventional systems and are widely used.[[j]](#cite_note-263)[[254]](#cite_note-264)[[255]](#cite_note-265) In rural areas, village transport services known as *angdes (angkutan pedesaan)* help connect smaller communities to cities.[[256]](#cite_note-266)

Railways operate mainly on Java and Sumatra,[[257]](#cite_note-267) with more [recent additions in Sulawesi](/wiki/Trans-Sulawesi_Railway "Trans-Sulawesi Railway"),[[258]](#cite_note-268) serving freight and passenger transport, including commuter and inter-city rail services like those in [Greater Jakarta](/wiki/KRL_Commuterline "KRL Commuterline") and [Yogyakarta](/wiki/KAI_Commuter_Yogyakarta_Line "KAI Commuter Yogyakarta Line"). [Rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") systems were introduced in Jakarta and [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang") in the late 2010s,[[249]](#cite_note-railways-258) and in 2023, Indonesia opened its first [high-speed rail line](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Indonesia "High-speed rail in Indonesia") (*Whoosh*) that links Jakarta and [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung "Bandung"), which is a product of collaboration with China.[[249]](#cite_note-railways-258)

Air and sea transport also play significant roles. [Soekarno–Hatta International Airport](/wiki/Soekarno%E2%80%93Hatta_International_Airport "Soekarno–Hatta International Airport"), Indonesia's largest, [served 54 million passengers in 2024](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic"), followed by [Ngurah Rai](/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_International_Airport "Ngurah Rai International Airport") and [Juanda International Airports](/wiki/Juanda_International_Airport "Juanda International Airport").[[259]](#cite_note-HVS-269) Flag carrier [Garuda Indonesia](/wiki/Garuda_Indonesia "Garuda Indonesia") is one of the world's few 5-star airlines and is a member of the airline alliance [SkyTeam](/wiki/SkyTeam "SkyTeam").[[260]](#cite_note-270)[[261]](#cite_note-271) The [Port of Tanjung Priok](/wiki/Port_of_Tanjung_Priok "Port of Tanjung Priok"), the country's largest and one of the busiest in the region,[[262]](#cite_note-272) handles over 50% of Indonesia's trans-shipment cargo traffic.[[263]](#cite_note-273)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Indonesia](/wiki/Energy_in_Indonesia "Energy in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/PLTB-Sidrap.jpg/250px-PLTB-Sidrap.jpg)](/wiki/File:PLTB-Sidrap.jpg)

Sidrap wind farm, Indonesia's first wind power plant,[[264]](#cite_note-274) in [Sidrap Regency](/wiki/Sidrap_Regency "Sidrap Regency"), [South Sulawesi](/wiki/South_Sulawesi "South Sulawesi")

Indonesia is a [major energy producer and consumer](/wiki/Energy_in_Indonesia "Energy in Indonesia") due to its large population, industrial base and resource endowment.[[k]](#cite_note-276) Total [installed power generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") capacity in 2023 stands at 70.8 gigawatts (GW),[[265]](#cite_note-EIA-275) with coal forming the largest source of power.[[266]](#cite_note-277)[[267]](#cite_note-278) Other significant sources include natural gas, oil and renewables such as geothermal, hydropower and solar.[[268]](#cite_note-energyADB-279) The state-owned [State Electricity Company](/wiki/Perusahaan_Listrik_Negara "Perusahaan Listrik Negara") (*Perusahaan Listrik Negara*, PLN) holds a monopoly on power generation, transmission and distribution across the country.[[269]](#cite_note-280)

Indonesia's energy mix remains dependent on non-renewable sources, particularly coal and followed by natural gas and oil.[[270]](#cite_note-NZE-281) Renewables, including geothermal, hydropower and solar account for a limited but gradually increasing share.[[271]](#cite_note-282) The potential for renewable energy is substantial, particularly geothermal, where the country ranks as one of the world's largest producers.[[268]](#cite_note-energyADB-279) The country is an important exporter of energy commodities, including coal and liquefied natural gas,[[272]](#cite_note-283)[[222]](#cite_note-OEC-231)[[273]](#cite_note-284) while also importing refined petroleum products.[[265]](#cite_note-EIA-275)

The government has outlined plans to diversify energy sources and increase the share of renewables as part of longer-term efforts to reduce emissions.[[274]](#cite_note-285)[[270]](#cite_note-NZE-281) However, the country has insufficient infrastructure for renewable energy, struggles to provide electricity to remote areas,[[275]](#cite_note-286) and continues to rely heavily on coal.[[276]](#cite_note-287)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Indonesia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Indonesia "Demographics of Indonesia") and [Indonesians](/wiki/Indonesians "Indonesians")

See also: [List of Indonesian cities by population](/wiki/List_of_Indonesian_cities_by_population "List of Indonesian cities by population") and [List of metropolitan areas in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Indonesia "List of metropolitan areas in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Population_density_of_Indonesia_by_district_%28kecamatan%29_%282022%29_%28alternate_colour_scheme%29.svg/500px-Population_density_of_Indonesia_by_district_%28kecamatan%29_%282022%29_%28alternate_colour_scheme%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_of_Indonesia_by_district_(kecamatan)_(2022)_(alternate_colour_scheme).svg)

A map of districts (*kecamatan*) coloured by population density as measured by person per square kilometres

Indonesia has a large and regionally diverse population shaped by its geography, history, and patterns of migration. With a population of 270.2 million according to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_Indonesian_census "2020 Indonesian census"),[[5]](#cite_note-2020census-6) Indonesia ranks as the world's [fourth most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population") behind India, China and the United States. Java, the world's most populated island, is home to 56% of the population.[[5]](#cite_note-2020census-6) The overall population density stands at 141 people per square kilometre (370 people/mi2),[[5]](#cite_note-2020census-6) but Java's density is significantly higher, reaching 1,171 people per square kilometre (3,030 people/mi2).[[277]](#cite_note-288) Indonesia's first post-colonial census in 1961 recorded a population of 97 million,[[278]](#cite_note-289) and projections estimate it will grow to 335 million by 2050.[[279]](#cite_note-290) The country maintains a relatively young demographic, with a median age of 31.5 years as of 2024.[[3]](#cite_note-CIA-4)

Population distribution is highly uneven, reflecting the country's diverse geography and [varying levels of development](/wiki/List_of_Indonesian_provinces_by_Human_Development_Index "List of Indonesian provinces by Human Development Index").[[280]](#cite_note-291) It ranges from the bustling [megacity](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity") of Jakarta to remote and [uncontacted tribes](/wiki/Uncontacted_peoples "Uncontacted peoples") in Papua.[[281]](#cite_note-292) As of 2024, approximately 59% of Indonesians live in urban areas,[[282]](#cite_note-293) with Jakarta as the country's [primate city](/wiki/Primate_city "Primate city") and the world's [most populous urban area](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities"), housing nearly 42 million people according to the United Nations.[[283]](#cite_note-294) In addition, about 8 million [Indonesians reside overseas](/wiki/Indonesian_diaspora "Indonesian diaspora"), with large communities in Malaysia, the Netherlands, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, and Taiwan.[[284]](#cite_note-295)
