### Ethnic groups and languages

Main articles: [Ethnic groups in Indonesia](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Indonesia "Ethnic groups in Indonesia"), [Native Indonesians](/wiki/Native_Indonesians "Native Indonesians"), and [Languages of Indonesia](/wiki/Languages_of_Indonesia "Languages of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Indonesia_Ethnic_Groups_Map_-_EN.svg/500px-Indonesia_Ethnic_Groups_Map_-_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Indonesia_Ethnic_Groups_Map_-_EN.svg)

A map of ethnic groups in Indonesia

Indonesia is home to around 600 distinct native ethnic groups,[[285]](#cite_note-BPS-296) predominantly descended from [Austronesian peoples](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") speaking [Proto-Austronesian languages](/wiki/Proto-Austronesian_language "Proto-Austronesian language"), likely from modern-day Taiwan.[[286]](#cite_note-297) The [Melanesians](/wiki/Melanesians "Melanesians"), who inhabit eastern Indonesia, represent another significant ethnic grouping.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor20035–7-30)[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitton2003139,_181,_251,_435-298)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee20066-29) The Javanese, making up 40% of the population,[[288]](#cite_note-ISEASdemo-299) are the largest ethnic group and the politically dominant one,[[289]](#cite_note-300) primarily residing in central and eastern Java, with sizeable numbers in other provinces. Other major groups include the [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people"), [Malay](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians"), [Batak](/wiki/Batak "Batak"), [Madurese](/wiki/Madurese_people "Madurese people"), [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people"), [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people"), and [Bugis](/wiki/Bugis_people "Bugis people").[[288]](#cite_note-ISEASdemo-299)[[l]](#cite_note-301) A sense of Indonesian nationhood exists alongside strong regional identities.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs1991256-302)

The official language, [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), is a standardized variety of [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") based on its [prestige dialect](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") originating around the Riau-Johor region, which became the archipelago's *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* over the course of centuries.[[291]](#cite_note-303)[[m]](#cite_note-306) It was first [promoted by nationalists in the 1920s](/wiki/Youth_Pledge "Youth Pledge") and gained official status in 1945 under the name *Bahasa Indonesia*; it is written in the Latin script and has since been widely adopted through its use in education, media, business, and governance.[[294]](#cite_note-307) While nearly all Indonesians speak *Bahasa*, most also speak one of over 700 local languages,[[295]](#cite_note-sevenhund-308) some of which retain distinct writing traditions.[[296]](#cite_note-309) These are predominantly from the [Austronesian family](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages"), with more than 150 [Papuan languages](/wiki/Papuan_languages "Papuan languages") in eastern Indonesia.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee20067-310) [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") is the most widely spoken local language[[295]](#cite_note-sevenhund-308) and holds co-official status in [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Special_Region_of_Yogyakarta "Special Region of Yogyakarta").[[298]](#cite_note-311)

The [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") and other European-descended populations like the [Indos](/wiki/Indo_people "Indo people"), though significant during colonial times, always represented a small fraction of the population, numbering only around 200,000 in 1930.[[299]](#cite_note-312) The Dutch language never gained substantial traction due to the Dutch colonial focus on commerce rather than cultural integration.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBakerPrys_Jones1998202-313)[[301]](#cite_note-314) Dutch fluency exists today in small numbers among some older generations and legal professionals,[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmmonDittmarMattheierTrudgill20062017-315) as specific legal codes remain available only in that language.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooij19992-316)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Indonesia](/wiki/Religion_in_Indonesia "Religion in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Religious_affiliation_by_district_%28kecamatan%29_in_Indonesia_%282022%29.svg/500px-Religious_affiliation_by_district_%28kecamatan%29_in_Indonesia_%282022%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Religious_affiliation_by_district_(kecamatan)_in_Indonesia_(2022).svg)

A map of districts (*kecamatan*) coloured by plurality/majority religious affiliation and what percentage of citizens it represents

Indonesia officially recognises [six religions](/wiki/Religion_in_Indonesia "Religion in Indonesia"): [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Indonesia "Islam in Indonesia"), [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Indonesia "Protestantism in Indonesia"), [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Indonesia "Roman Catholicism in Indonesia"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Indonesia "Hinduism in Indonesia"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Indonesia "Buddhism in Indonesia"), and [Confucianism](/wiki/Supreme_Council_for_the_Confucian_Religion_in_Indonesia "Supreme Council for the Confucian Religion in Indonesia"),[[304]](#cite_note-317)[[305]](#cite_note-Marshall-318) while acknowledging religious freedom in the constitution.[[306]](#cite_note-319)[[11]](#cite_note-UUD45-13) As of 2024, 87.1% of the population (244 million Indonesians) are Muslims,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) making Indonesia the world's most populous Muslim-majority country,[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001379-320) with [Sunnis](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") constituting 99% of the Muslim population.[[308]](#cite_note-321)[[n]](#cite_note-323) Christians, comprising 10% of the population,[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) form majorities in several eastern provinces,[[310]](#cite_note-HCI-324) while Hindus and Buddhists are primarily Balinese and Chinese Indonesians, respectively.[[311]](#cite_note-McDaniel-325)[[312]](#cite_note-326)

Before the arrival of major world religions, Indonesia's native peoples practised [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism") and [dynamism](/wiki/Dynamism_(metaphysics) "Dynamism (metaphysics)"), worshipping ancestral spirits and believing in the supernatural (*[hyang](/wiki/Hyang "Hyang")*) inhabiting natural elements, such as large trees, mountains and forests.[[313]](#cite_note-Ooi-327) Such beliefs are common to the [Austronesian peoples](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples").[[313]](#cite_note-Ooi-327) These indigenous traditions, such as Sundanese [Sunda Wiwitan](/wiki/Sunda_Wiwitan "Sunda Wiwitan"),[[314]](#cite_note-328) Javanese [Kejawèn](/wiki/Kejaw%C3%A8n "Kejawèn"),[[315]](#cite_note-329) and Dayak's [Kaharingan](/wiki/Kaharingan "Kaharingan"),[[316]](#cite_note-330) have profoundly influenced modern religious practices, resulting in a less orthodox and syncretic form of faith like Javanese [abangan](/wiki/Abangan "Abangan"), [Balinese Hinduism](/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism") and Dayak Christianity.[[317]](#cite_note-331)[[318]](#cite_note-332)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Banda_Aceh%27s_Grand_Mosque%2C_Indonesia.jpg/250px-Banda_Aceh%27s_Grand_Mosque%2C_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banda_Aceh%27s_Grand_Mosque,_Indonesia.jpg)

[Baiturrahman Grand Mosque](/wiki/Baiturrahman_Grand_Mosque "Baiturrahman Grand Mosque") in [Banda Aceh](/wiki/Banda_Aceh "Banda Aceh"), [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh "Aceh"). The [spread of Islam in Indonesia](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam_in_Indonesia "Spread of Islam in Indonesia") began in the region.

Hinduism reached the archipelago in the 1st century CE,[[311]](#cite_note-McDaniel-325) followed by Buddhism in the 5th century.[[319]](#cite_note-333) Through empires such as Majapahit, Srivijaya, and Sailendra, both religions helped shape Indonesia’s religious history and left lasting cultural influences that endure today, even though neither is a majority faith.[[320]](#cite_note-334)[[321]](#cite_note-335) Islam arrived as early as the 8th century[[322]](#cite_note-336) through Sunni and Sufi traders from the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and [southern Arabian peninsula](/wiki/South_Arabia "South Arabia"), mixing with local cultural and religious traditions to form a distinct Islamic culture (*[santri](/wiki/Pesantren "Pesantren")*).[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs199112–14-42)[[323]](#cite_note-337) By the 16th century, Islam had become the dominant religion in Java and Sumatra, resulting from the blend of trade, [*dawah*](/wiki/Islamic_missionary_activity "Islamic missionary activity"), such as by the [Wali Sanga](/wiki/Wali_Sanga "Wali Sanga") and Chinese explorer [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He"),[[324]](#cite_note-338) and military campaigns by several sultanates.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs200117,_36–57-339)[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2008-340)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Salah_Satu_Upacara_Besar_Di_Pura_Agung_Besakih.jpg/250px-Salah_Satu_Upacara_Besar_Di_Pura_Agung_Besakih.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salah_Satu_Upacara_Besar_Di_Pura_Agung_Besakih.jpg)

A Hindu prayer ceremony at [Besakih Temple](/wiki/Besakih_Temple "Besakih Temple") in [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali"), the only province where [Hinduism](/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism") is the predominant religion

Catholicism and Protestantism were later introduced through missionary efforts during European colonisation, such as by [Francis Xavier](/wiki/Francis_Xavier "Francis Xavier"),[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs200128–29-341)[[328]](#cite_note-342) though the spread of the former encountered difficulties under the VOC and Dutch colonial eras.[[329]](#cite_note-343)[[330]](#cite_note-344) The latter's primary branches include the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"),[[310]](#cite_note-HCI-324) though a multitude of other denominations exist in the country.[[331]](#cite_note-345) A small Jewish presence has existed in the archipelago, primarily descendants of Dutch and Iraqi Jews,[[332]](#cite_note-346) though their numbers have dwindled since independence in 1945.[[333]](#cite_note-347) Only a few Jews remain today, mostly in major cities like Jakarta, [Manado](/wiki/Manado "Manado") and Surabaya.[[334]](#cite_note-Jews-348) One of the remaining synagogues, [Sha'ar Hashamayim](/wiki/Sha%27ar_Hashamayim_Synagogue_(Tondano) "Sha'ar Hashamayim Synagogue (Tondano)"), is located in [Tondano](/wiki/Tondano "Tondano"), around 31 km from Manado.[[334]](#cite_note-Jews-348)[[335]](#cite_note-349)

Religion is central to the lives of the overwhelming majority of Indonesians, reflecting its integral role in the country's society, culture, and identity.[[336]](#cite_note-350)[[337]](#cite_note-351) Interfaith relations are significantly shaped by political leadership and civil society, guided by the first principle of Pancasila, which emphasises belief in a supreme deity and religious tolerance.[[338]](#cite_note-352) While it promotes harmony,[[339]](#cite_note-353) religious intolerance continues to be a recurring issue.[[83]](#cite_note-RIP-87)[[198]](#cite_note-mino-207) Followers of [indigenous religions](/wiki/Indigenous_religion "Indigenous religion"), officially called *aliran kepercayaan* or cultural belief systems, have been subject to a lack of legal and governmental recognition that leads to discrimination as well as uncertainty regarding their exact numbers.[[305]](#cite_note-Marshall-318)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Indonesia](/wiki/Education_in_Indonesia "Education in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/No_18_Rektorat_Universitas_Indonesia.jpg/250px-No_18_Rektorat_Universitas_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:No_18_Rektorat_Universitas_Indonesia.jpg)

[University of Indonesia](/wiki/University_of_Indonesia "University of Indonesia") is one of Indonesia's top universities.

Indonesia has [one of the largest education systems](/wiki/Education_in_Indonesia "Education in Indonesia") in the world, with over 50 million students, 4 million teachers, and more than 250,000 schools.[[340]](#cite_note-worldBankEdu-354) Overseen by several government ministries,[[o]](#cite_note-355) the system follows a 6-3-3-4 structure: six years of elementary school, three years each of junior and senior secondary school, and four years of tertiary education.[[341]](#cite_note-356) While the literacy rate is high (96%),[[3]](#cite_note-CIA-4) it is lower in rural areas.[[342]](#cite_note-357) Enrolment rates vary across educational levels, with near-universal enrolment in primary education (97.9%), but drop to 81.7% and 64.2% in lower and upper secondary education and around 42.6% for tertiary education.[[343]](#cite_note-358)[[344]](#cite_note-359)

Government spending on education accounted for approximately 1.3% of GDP in 2023.[[345]](#cite_note-360) In 2022, there were 4,481 higher education institutions in the country, including universities, Islamic institutions, and open universities.[[346]](#cite_note-361) Among these, the [University of Indonesia](/wiki/University_of_Indonesia "University of Indonesia"), [Gadjah Mada University](/wiki/Gadjah_Mada_University "Gadjah Mada University"), and the [Bandung Institute of Technology](/wiki/Bandung_Institute_of_Technology "Bandung Institute of Technology") stand out as the country's leading institutions, all ranking within the world's top 300 universities.[[347]](#cite_note-362)

Issues regarding quality and equity are persistent, particularly urban–rural disparities, inadequate infrastructure, and a lack of qualified teachers, whose wages are lower than those in neighbouring countries.[[348]](#cite_note-363)[[349]](#cite_note-364) The system also lags behind international benchmarks, such as the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA), where Indonesian students consistently rank near the bottom in reading, mathematics, and science.[[350]](#cite_note-365) The higher education sector has been struggling with underfunding, low quality, limited research output and a mismatch between graduates' skills and labour market needs.[[351]](#cite_note-366)[[352]](#cite_note-367)

### Healthcare

Main article: [Healthcare in Indonesia](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Indonesia "Healthcare in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Ciptomangun-hospital.jpg/250px-Ciptomangun-hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ciptomangun-hospital.jpg)

[Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo Hospital](/wiki/Dr._Cipto_Mangunkusumo_Hospital "Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo Hospital") in Jakarta

Indonesia has made significant progress in developing its [healthcare system](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Indonesia "Healthcare in Indonesia") since 1945. Initially, healthcare services were limited, with a shortage of doctors, hospitals, and infrastructure.[[353]](#cite_note-APO-368) In the late 1960s, the government began establishing [community health centres](/wiki/Puskesmas "Puskesmas") (*puskesmas*) to provide basic services in rural areas.[[353]](#cite_note-APO-368) With the help of the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") in the 1970s and 1980s, Indonesia implemented an immunisation program to combat diseases like polio and measles.[[354]](#cite_note-BMC-369) The system experienced a major transformation in 2014 with the launch of *[Jaminan Kesehatan Nasional](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Indonesia#Universal_health_coverage "Healthcare in Indonesia")* (JKN), a [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") managed by the [Social Security Agency on Health](/wiki/BPJS_Kesehatan "BPJS Kesehatan") (*BPJS Kesehatan*).[[355]](#cite_note-370) It is one of the world's largest [single-payer systems](/wiki/Single-payer_healthcare "Single-payer healthcare"), covering over 95% of the population (265 million people) by 2023.[[356]](#cite_note-371)

Government spending on healthcare accounted for 2.69% of GDP in 2022.[[357]](#cite_note-372) Primary healthcare is delivered through *puskesmas*, hospitals, and private clinics.[[353]](#cite_note-APO-368) While the healthcare system lags behind those in ASEAN neighbours like Malaysia and Singapore,[[358]](#cite_note-373) significant public health outcomes have been achieved, such as an increase in life expectancy (from 54.9 years in 1973 to 71.1 years in 2023),[[359]](#cite_note-374) a decline in child mortality (from 15.5 deaths per 100 live births in 1972 to 2.1 deaths in 2022),[[360]](#cite_note-375) polio eradication in 2014,[[354]](#cite_note-BMC-369) and decreasing cases of malaria.[[361]](#cite_note-376)

Some chronic health issues persist, including [child stunting](/wiki/Stunted_growth "Stunted growth") that affects 21.6% of children under five according to a 2022 data.[[362]](#cite_note-377) Low air quality, particularly in major cities,[[363]](#cite_note-378) contributes to respiratory illnesses, while maternal and child health indicators remain areas of concern, with a [maternal mortality](/wiki/Maternal_death "Maternal death") rate the third highest in the region.[[364]](#cite_note-379) Additionally, Indonesia has one of the [highest smoking rates](/wiki/Smoking_in_Indonesia "Smoking in Indonesia") globally (34.8% of adults), contributing to a high prevalence of non-communicable diseases like cardiovascular issues and lung cancer.[[365]](#cite_note-380)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Indonesia](/wiki/Culture_of_Indonesia "Culture of Indonesia")

See also: [National Intangible Cultural Heritage of Indonesia](/wiki/National_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_of_Indonesia "National Intangible Cultural Heritage of Indonesia") and [Public holidays in Indonesia](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Indonesia "Public holidays in Indonesia")

Indonesia's culture reflects more than two millennia of interaction between indigenous traditions and external influences. Its cultural development has been shaped by Austronesian and Melanesian heritage, as well as sustained contact with the Indian subcontinent, China, the Middle East, and Europe through trade, migration, and colonisation.[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee20068–9-381)[[367]](#cite_note-382) These interactions have produced a society characterised by diversity in culture, language and ethnicity,[[295]](#cite_note-sevenhund-308)[[285]](#cite_note-BPS-296) including artistic expression and social practice.

Indonesia currently has [16 items recognised by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists"), including [wayang](/wiki/Wayang "Wayang") puppet theatre, [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik"), [angklung](/wiki/Angklung "Angklung"), the [saman dance](/wiki/Saman_dance "Saman dance"), and [pencak silat](/wiki/Pencak_silat "Pencak silat"), with recent joint nominations adding [pantun](/wiki/Pantun "Pantun"), [kebaya](/wiki/Kebaya "Kebaya"), and [kolintang](/wiki/Kolintang "Kolintang") to the list.[[368]](#cite_note-383)

### Art and architecture

Main articles: [Indonesian art](/wiki/Indonesian_art "Indonesian art") and [Architecture of Indonesia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Indonesia "Architecture of Indonesia")

Further information: [Indonesian painting](/wiki/Indonesian_painting "Indonesian painting")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Raden_Saleh_-_Diponegoro_arrest.jpg/330px-Raden_Saleh_-_Diponegoro_arrest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raden_Saleh_-_Diponegoro_arrest.jpg)

*[The Arrest of Pangeran Diponegoro](/wiki/The_Arrest_of_Pangeran_Diponegoro "The Arrest of Pangeran Diponegoro")* (1857) by [Raden Saleh](/wiki/Raden_Saleh "Raden Saleh")

Indonesian visual arts encompass a wide range of traditional and contemporary forms that reflect regional diversity and historical exchange.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee20067–9,_52–67-384) Bali's [artistic traditions](/wiki/Balinese_art "Balinese art"), such as classical [Kamasan](/wiki/Kamasan "Kamasan") and [Wayang](/wiki/Wayang "Wayang")-style painting, are renowned, originating from visual narratives depicted on [candi](/wiki/Candi_of_Indonesia "Candi of Indonesia") bas-reliefs from eastern Java.[[370]](#cite_note-385) Traditional architecture varies significantly among ethnic groups and regions, with iconic and traditional houses (*[rumah adat](/wiki/Rumah_adat "Rumah adat")*) like Toraja's *[Tongkonan](/wiki/Tongkonan "Tongkonan")*, Minangkabau's *[Rumah Gadang](/wiki/Rumah_Gadang "Rumah Gadang")*, Java's *[Pendopo](/wiki/Pendopo "Pendopo")*, and [Dayak](/wiki/Dayak_people "Dayak people") longhouses each showcasing unique local customs and histories.[[371]](#cite_note-386)

[Megalithic sculptures](/wiki/Megalithic_art "Megalithic art") discovered in parts of Sumatra, Sulawesi, and eastern Indonesia illustrate tribal arts among communities such as the Nias, Batak, Asmat, Dayak, and Toraja peoples.[[25]](#cite_note-Tara-28)[[372]](#cite_note-387)[[373]](#cite_note-388) From the 8th to the 15th centuries, the Javanese civilisation excelled in sophisticated stone sculpting and architecture, heavily influenced by the Hindu-Buddhist Dharmic culture.[[374]](#cite_note-389) This period produced monumental works like the [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") and [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") temples.[[375]](#cite_note-390)[[376]](#cite_note-391) both of which are emblematic of Indonesia's cultural heritage.

### Music, dance and clothing

Main articles: [Music of Indonesia](/wiki/Music_of_Indonesia "Music of Indonesia"), [Dance in Indonesia](/wiki/Dance_in_Indonesia "Dance in Indonesia"), and [National costume of Indonesia](/wiki/National_costume_of_Indonesia "National costume of Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Katoenen_wikkelrok_met_geometrisch_patroon_TMnr_5713-2.jpg/250px-COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Katoenen_wikkelrok_met_geometrisch_patroon_TMnr_5713-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Katoenen_wikkelrok_met_geometrisch_patroon_TMnr_5713-2.jpg)

An Indonesian [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik")

Music and dance play an important role in Indonesian cultural life and have long been associated with ritual, storytelling, and communal gatherings. Indigenous musical traditions predate written records, with tribes employing instruments like the [angklung](/wiki/Angklung "Angklung"), [gamelan](/wiki/Gamelan "Gamelan"), and [sasando](/wiki/Sasando "Sasando"). Over time, external influences enriched Indonesian music, such as the [gambus](/wiki/Qanb%C5%ABs "Qanbūs") and [qasida](/wiki/Qasida "Qasida") from the Middle East,[[377]](#cite_note-392) [keroncong](/wiki/Kroncong "Kroncong") from Portugal,[[378]](#cite_note-393) and [dangdut](/wiki/Dangdut "Dangdut"), which incorporates Hindi, Malay, and Middle Eastern elements.[[379]](#cite_note-394) Today, Indonesian music enjoys regional popularity in Malaysia and Singapore due to cultural similarities and [language intelligibility](/wiki/Comparison_of_Indonesian_and_Standard_Malay "Comparison of Indonesian and Standard Malay").[[380]](#cite_note-395)[[381]](#cite_note-396)

Indonesia is home to more than 3,000 traditional dances, many of which originated in rituals and religious worship,[[382]](#cite_note-397) such as the dance of witch doctors and [Hudoq](/wiki/Hudoq "Hudoq"), as well as periods of Hindu-Buddhist and Islamic influence. In contemporary Indonesia, traditional dances like those of Java, Bali, and Dayak continue to be a living tradition[[383]](#cite_note-398)
amid the growing popularity of modern and urban dances shaped by Western and South Korean cultures, such as [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") and [K-pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop").[[384]](#cite_note-399)[[385]](#cite_note-400)

Clothing traditions also vary widely across the archipelago. *[Batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik")* and *[kebaya](/wiki/Kebaya "Kebaya")* are widely recognised as national attire, with predominant roots in Javanese culture.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee200665,_143-401) Traditional attire varies by region and province, such as the Batak [ulos](/wiki/Ulos "Ulos"), Malay and Minangkabau [songket](/wiki/Songket "Songket"), and Sasak [ikat](/wiki/Ikat "Ikat"), and is commonly worn for ceremonies, weddings, and formal events.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006138–147-402)

### Theatre and cinema

Main articles: [Cinema of Indonesia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Indonesia "Cinema of Indonesia") and [Theatre of Indonesia](/wiki/Theatre_of_Indonesia "Theatre of Indonesia")

Further information: [List of highest-grossing films in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_Indonesia "List of highest-grossing films in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Wayang_Wong_Bharata_Pandawa.jpg/250px-Wayang_Wong_Bharata_Pandawa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wayang_Wong_Bharata_Pandawa.jpg)

The *[Pandavas](/wiki/Pandava "Pandava")* and *[Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna")* in an act of the *Wayang Wong* performance

Traditional Indonesian theatre encompasses a variety of performance forms that combine storytelling, music, and visual art. *Wayang* shadow puppetry is one of the best-known forms, often depicting Hindu epics like the *Ramayana* and *Mahabharata*.[[388]](#cite_note-403) Other theatrical traditions include *[Ludruk](/wiki/Ludruk "Ludruk")*, *[Ketoprak](/wiki/Ketoprak "Ketoprak")*, *[Sandiwara](/wiki/Sandiwara "Sandiwara")*, *[Lenong](/wiki/Lenong "Lenong")*,[[389]](#cite_note-404) and Balinese dance dramas, which often incorporate humour, music, and audience interaction.[[390]](#cite_note-405) Regional performance traditions reflect local histories and values like the Minangkabau *[Randai](/wiki/Randai "Randai")*, which combines music, dance, and martial arts (*[silat](/wiki/Silat "Silat")*) to recount legends and historical narratives.[[391]](#cite_note-406) In the modern period, theatre groups such as *Teater Koma*, have used satire to address social and political themes.[[392]](#cite_note-407)[[393]](#cite_note-408)

Indonesia's film industry began during the Dutch colonial period with the release of *[Loetoeng Kasaroeng](/wiki/Loetoeng_Kasaroeng "Loetoeng Kasaroeng")* (1926)[[394]](#cite_note-INACine-409) and it expanded post-independence with [Usmar Ismail](/wiki/Usmar_Ismail "Usmar Ismail")'s pioneering work in the 1950s.[[394]](#cite_note-INACine-409)[[395]](#cite_note-410) During the latter part of the Sukarno era in the 1960s, films were used to promote nationalism and anti-Western sentiment,[[394]](#cite_note-INACine-409) while Suharto's New Order imposed [censorship](/wiki/Censorship_in_Indonesia "Censorship in Indonesia") to maintain social order.[[396]](#cite_note-Krishna_Sen-411) Film productions peaked in the 1980s with notable titles such as *[Pengabdi Setan](/wiki/Satan%27s_Slave_(1980_film) "Satan's Slave (1980 film)")* (1980), *[Tjoet Nja' Dhien](/wiki/Tjoet_Nja%27_Dhien "Tjoet Nja' Dhien")* (1988) and *[Warkop](/wiki/Warkop "Warkop")* comedy films, but the industry declined in the 1990s.[[397]](#cite_note-brill-412)

In the post-Suharto era, the industry saw a resurgence.[[398]](#cite_note-413) Independent filmmakers tackled previously censored themes like race, religion, and love,[[396]](#cite_note-Krishna_Sen-411) producing notable films such as *Kuldesak* (1999) and *[Ada Apa dengan Cinta?](/wiki/Ada_Apa_dengan_Cinta%3F "Ada Apa dengan Cinta?")* (2002).[[397]](#cite_note-brill-412) The [Indonesian Film Festival](/wiki/Indonesian_Film_Festival "Indonesian Film Festival") (*Festival Film Indonesia*), which gives out the [Citra Award](/wiki/Citra_Award "Citra Award"), has celebrated cinematic achievements since 1955.[[399]](#cite_note-414)

### Literature and mass media

Main articles: [Indonesian literature](/wiki/Indonesian_literature "Indonesian literature") and [Mass media in Indonesia](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Indonesia "Mass media in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Pramudya_Ananta_Tur_Kesusastraan_Modern_Indonesia_p226.jpg/250px-Pramudya_Ananta_Tur_Kesusastraan_Modern_Indonesia_p226.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pramudya_Ananta_Tur_Kesusastraan_Modern_Indonesia_p226.jpg)

[Pramoedya Ananta Toer](/wiki/Pramoedya_Ananta_Toer "Pramoedya Ananta Toer") was considered to be Southeast Asia's leading candidate for a [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[400]](#cite_note-415)

Media and literary traditions in Indonesia reflect both historical continuity and technological change. Early literature include [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") inscriptions from the 5th century and a strong [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"),[[401]](#cite_note-416) later expressed through written forms such as *[syair](/wiki/Syair "Syair")*, *[pantun](/wiki/Pantun "Pantun")*, *[hikayat](/wiki/Hikayat "Hikayat")*, and *[babad](/wiki/Babad "Babad")*.[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee200667–70-417) Notable works in this category include *[Hikayat Hang Tuah](/wiki/Hikayat_Hang_Tuah "Hikayat Hang Tuah")* and *[Babad Tanah Jawi](/wiki/Babad_Tanah_Jawi "Babad Tanah Jawi")*. The establishment of *[Balai Pustaka](/wiki/Balai_Pustaka "Balai Pustaka")* in 1917 marked a push to develop indigenous literature, leading to a literary Golden Age in the 1950s and 1960s.[[403]](#cite_note-literary-418) Early modern literature originates in the Sumatran tradition[[404]](#cite_note-419)[[405]](#cite_note-420) and has been shaped by political and social change,[[403]](#cite_note-literary-418)[[406]](#cite_note-421) producing works from notable figures like [Chairil Anwar](/wiki/Chairil_Anwar "Chairil Anwar"), [Pramoedya Ananta Toer](/wiki/Pramoedya_Ananta_Toer "Pramoedya Ananta Toer"), and [Ayu Utami](/wiki/Ayu_Utami "Ayu Utami").[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicklefs2001264-422)

[Media](/wiki/Media_of_Indonesia "Media of Indonesia") freedom in Indonesia significantly improved after the fall of Suharto's rule, during which the Ministry of Information tightly controlled the media.[[408]](#cite_note-423) The early years of the post-Suharto era was marked by a proliferation of print and electronic media amid greater press freedom.[[409]](#cite_note-424)[[410]](#cite_note-425) Internet use, which began in the early 1990s after the first Internet service provider went commercial,[[411]](#cite_note-426) grew rapidly after 2000[[412]](#cite_note-427) and began transforming patters of media consumption, which had become evident during the 2010s.[[413]](#cite_note-428) By 2023, the country had 210 million internet users, with mobile phones as the primary point of access.[[414]](#cite_note-429)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Indonesian cuisine](/wiki/Indonesian_cuisine "Indonesian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Nasi_ramas_rendang.JPG/250px-Nasi_ramas_rendang.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nasi_ramas_rendang.JPG)

*[Nasi Padang](/wiki/Nasi_Padang "Nasi Padang")* with *[rendang](/wiki/Rendang "Rendang")*, *[gulai](/wiki/Gulai "Gulai")*, and vegetables is one of the [Minangkabau cuisines](/wiki/Padang_cuisine "Padang cuisine").

Indonesian cuisine reflects the country's geographic diversity, cultural plurality, and long history of external contact. It comprises numerous regional traditions shaped by indigenous practices and foreign influences, including Chinese, Middle Eastern, Indian, African, and European cuisines.[[415]](#cite_note-430) Rice is the primary [staple food](/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food") across much of the archipelago and is typically served with side dishes of meat, vegetables, or fish.[[416]](#cite_note-WijayaS-431) Fundamental ingredients include spices—particularly chilli—along with [coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk"),[[417]](#cite_note-432) fish, and chicken, which contribute to the distinctive flavour profiles found across different regions.[[416]](#cite_note-WijayaS-431)

Some popular dishes, such as *[nasi goreng](/wiki/Nasi_goreng "Nasi goreng")*, *[gado-gado](/wiki/Gado-gado "Gado-gado")*, *[mie](/wiki/Noodle "Noodle")*, and *[sate](/wiki/Satay "Satay")*, are widely consumed throughout the country.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006131–133-433) Regional cuisines remain strongly associated with local identities, with [Minangkabau dishes](/wiki/Padang_cuisine "Padang cuisine") like *[rendang](/wiki/Rendang "Rendang")*[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006132-434) gaining international recognition.[[416]](#cite_note-WijayaS-431) Fermented foods also form part of Indonesian culinary traditions, including *[oncom](/wiki/Oncom "Oncom")*, which is produced using various fungal cultures and is especially prevalent in West Java.[[420]](#cite_note-435) In 2014, the Ministry of Tourism designated *[tumpeng](/wiki/Tumpeng "Tumpeng")* as an icon of Indonesian traditional cuisine, symbolising the nation's diverse food cultures.[[421]](#cite_note-436)

### Sports

Main articles: [Sports in Indonesia](/wiki/Sports_in_Indonesia "Sports in Indonesia") and [Indonesian martial arts](/wiki/Indonesian_martial_arts "Indonesian martial arts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Pencak_Silat_Betawi_1.jpg/250px-Pencak_Silat_Betawi_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pencak_Silat_Betawi_1.jpg)

A demonstration of *[pencak silat](/wiki/Pencak_silat "Pencak silat")*, a form of martial arts

Sports in Indonesia include both internationally competitive disciplines and traditional activities. [Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in terms of nationwide engagement and interest.[[422]](#cite_note-soccer-437) The [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Indonesia) "Super League (Indonesia)") functions as the country's top-tier competition. [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team") was the first Asian representative to appear at the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), taking part in the [1938 tournament](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") as the Dutch East Indies.[[422]](#cite_note-soccer-437) Despite its popularity, the national program has only achieved limited success at the regional level, such as the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games") (SEA Games).[[423]](#cite_note-438)

[Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") has produced Indonesia's most notable international sporting results.[[424]](#cite_note-439) The country is among the few to have won both the [Thomas](/wiki/Thomas_Cup "Thomas Cup") and [Uber Cups](/wiki/Uber_Cup "Uber Cup"), the world team championship of men's and women's badminton.[[425]](#cite_note-440) Together with [weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting"), badminton contributes the largest share of [Indonesia's Olympic gold medals](/wiki/Indonesia_at_the_Olympics "Indonesia at the Olympics").[[426]](#cite_note-441) Other popular sports include [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and basketball, which were featured in the country's multi-sport event [National Sports Week](/wiki/National_Sports_Week "National Sports Week") (*Pekan Olahraga Nasional*, PON) in 1948.[[427]](#cite_note-442)

Examples of traditional sports include *[sepak takraw](/wiki/Sepak_takraw "Sepak takraw")*, bull racing (*[karapan sapi](/wiki/Karapan_sapi "Karapan sapi")*) in [Madura](/wiki/Madura "Madura"),[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006176-443)[[429]](#cite_note-444) and ritual combat traditions, such as *caci* in [Flores](/wiki/Flores "Flores") and *[pasola](/wiki/Pasola "Pasola")* in [Sumba](/wiki/Sumba "Sumba").[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006174–175,_219-445) *[Pencak silat](/wiki/Pencak_silat "Pencak silat")* is an Indonesian martial art,[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForshee2006174-446) which became an official event at the 2018 Asian Games, where Indonesia emerged as one of the leading competitors.[[432]](#cite_note-447) In multi-sport regional competitions, Indonesia has topped the SEA Games medal table ten times since 1977,[[433]](#cite_note-448) and ranks second in the [all-time medal standings](/wiki/SEA_Games#All-time_medal_table "SEA Games") behind Thailand.[[434]](#cite_note-449)
